name: Wake
whyDescription: Booming Research Triangle tech corridor with strong income growth, manageable housing costs, and one of the highest job creation rates in the Southeast.

name: Travis
whyDescription: Austin remains a magnet for tech and startups with explosive job growth, though home prices have climbed sharply. No state income tax boosts take-home pay.

name: King
whyDescription: Seattle metro pairs world-class tech and aerospace employers with very high incomes, though housing affordability remains a major hurdle.

name: Denver
whyDescription: Diversified economy across tech, energy, and aerospace with strong mobility and lifestyle appeal, but home prices have outpaced wage gains.

name: Salt Lake
whyDescription: The "Silicon Slopes" hub balances strong tech-driven income growth with a tight labor market and better affordability than the West Coast.

name: Mecklenburg
whyDescription: Charlotte is the second-largest US banking hub with diverse employment, solid mobility, and home prices that remain reasonable for a major metro.

name: Ada
whyDescription: Boise has been one of the fastest growing metros in the country with rapidly expanding tech employment, though housing has gotten markedly more expensive.

name: Davidson
whyDescription: Nashville is a healthcare and music industry hub with no state income tax, strong job creation, and a lower cost of living than coastal peers.

name: Maricopa
whyDescription: Phoenix metro continues to attract semiconductor and logistics investment with growing wages and relatively affordable housing for a Sunbelt boomtown.

name: Middlesex
whyDescription: Greater Boston biotech and academic economy delivers very high incomes and economic mobility, balanced against expensive housing.

name: New York
whyDescription: Manhattan offers unrivaled access to finance, media, and professional services with the highest mobility ceiling in the country. Housing is the steepest tradeoff.

name: Los Angeles
whyDescription: A vast, diverse economy with strength in media, aerospace, and trade. Housing costs and longer unemployment spells drag on the wealth-building picture.

name: Cook
whyDescription: Chicago is the only major US metro pairing big-city career opportunity with genuinely affordable housing — though tax burden and job growth lag.

name: Harris
whyDescription: Houston pairs energy, healthcare, and port-driven employment with some of the most affordable big-city housing in the country and no state income tax.

name: San Francisco
whyDescription: Highest median income of any major US county and unrivaled tech mobility, but housing costs make actually building wealth difficult without an equity event.

name: Miami-Dade
whyDescription: A magnet for finance and crypto with no state income tax and strong job creation, though home prices have surged faster than local wages.

name: Dallas
whyDescription: DFW continues to attract corporate relocations and finance back-office growth with healthy wages, affordable housing, and zero state income tax.

name: Santa Clara
whyDescription: Silicon Valley delivers the highest tech compensation on earth but housing is the most expensive in the country — wealth here requires equity comp.

name: Fulton
whyDescription: Atlanta has emerged as a Black wealth and corporate HQ hub with strong income growth in tech, film, and finance, with reasonable housing for a major metro.

name: Allegheny
whyDescription: Pittsburgh offers some of the best housing affordability among large metros plus growing robotics and healthcare sectors — a value play for early savers.

name: Hamilton
whyDescription: Cincinnati pairs affordable housing with steady corporate employment (P&G, Kroger) and a low cost of living that stretches a starter salary.

name: Franklin
whyDescription: Columbus has quietly become one of the strongest Midwest growth stories with Intel investment, JPMorgan ops, and still-affordable housing.

name: Marion
whyDescription: Indianapolis is a logistics and insurance hub with extremely affordable housing — strong setup for early-career saving and home ownership.

name: Hennepin
whyDescription: The Twin Cities have a high concentration of Fortune 500 HQs, strong incomes, and surprisingly affordable housing for a major metro.

name: Johnson
whyDescription: Overland Park is one of the highest-income, lowest-poverty large counties in the Midwest with excellent schools and reasonable housing.

name: Boone
whyDescription: Columbia, MO offers low unemployment, a stable university-anchored economy, and very affordable housing — strong fundamentals for early savings.

name: Delaware
whyDescription: One of the highest-income suburban counties in Ohio with strong mobility, top schools, and home prices that remain reasonable for the income level.

name: Waukesha
whyDescription: Affluent Milwaukee suburb with low unemployment, strong manufacturing and finance employment, and affordable single-family housing.

name: Orange
whyDescription: OC delivers high incomes and a diversified economy, but coastal California housing costs make wealth accumulation slow without equity comp.

name: Riverside
whyDescription: Inland Empire offers slightly better housing than coastal CA with logistics-driven job growth, though wages and unemployment lag the coast.

name: Hillsborough
whyDescription: Tampa pairs strong income growth with no state income tax and a financial services boom, though housing prices have escalated quickly.

name: Pinellas
whyDescription: St. Petersburg / Clearwater offers Florida tax benefits and growing service-sector employment, with home prices rising but still below Miami.

name: Broward
whyDescription: Fort Lauderdale benefits from South Florida finance migration with no state income tax and growing professional services, balanced by rising housing.

name: Fairfield
whyDescription: Hedge fund corridor with very high incomes and strong mobility, though state tax burden and slow employment growth offset some of the benefit.

name: Westchester
whyDescription: High-income NYC suburb with strong professional services employment and excellent schools, but property taxes and housing weigh on net savings.

name: DuPage
whyDescription: Affluent Chicago suburb with high household incomes, strong corporate employment, and home prices that remain reasonable for the income level.

name: Oakland
whyDescription: Detroit-area auto and engineering hub with very strong income-to-home-price ratios — a quiet wealth-building corridor often overlooked.

name: Wayne
whyDescription: Detroit has the lowest housing costs of any major metro and improving mobility, but unemployment and income growth still lag national peers.

name: Cuyahoga
whyDescription: Cleveland offers extreme housing affordability and a growing healthcare anchor, but population decline and slow wage growth limit upside.

name: St. Louis City
whyDescription: Very affordable housing but elevated unemployment and persistent population loss make broad wealth building harder despite low cost of living.

name: Baltimore City
whyDescription: Affordable housing within reach of DC employment, but population decline and elevated unemployment temper the wealth-building math.

name: Erie
whyDescription: Some of the cheapest housing in the country, but a shrinking manufacturing base and limited job creation make moving here a gamble on remote work.

name: Lackawanna
whyDescription: Scranton has extreme housing affordability and proximity to NYC for hybrid workers, but local wage growth remains weak.

name: Jefferson
whyDescription: Birmingham has an emerging healthcare and biotech anchor at UAB plus low housing costs, but historical mobility scores remain modest.

name: Hinds
whyDescription: Jackson offers very low housing costs but lower incomes and persistent unemployment limit wealth accumulation despite cheap living.

name: Kern
whyDescription: Bakersfield offers California with cheaper housing, but oil-dependent employment cycles and high unemployment weigh on the outlook.
